one_line_takeaway: MATRIX embeds LLM-code provenance watermarks by solving parity-check equations over a dual-channel (variable-renaming + semantics-preserving-transformation) anchor state vector, achieving ~99.2% average detection TPR, near-zero (0-0.14%) functionality loss, 2-6x more watermarkable code than the ACW baseline, and materially better (though still imperfect, ~51-54% TPR) robustness against LLM-based rewriting attacks than prior black-box and white-box baselines.
key_finding: Detection accuracy (Table III): MATRIX achieves TPR of 99.64% (APPS-G), 99.39% (HumanEval-G), 100.00% (MBPP-G), 96.95% (HumanEval-S), 100.00% (MBPP-S), averaging ~99.20% per the abstract, versus ACW's 98.82/99.39/99.79/96.95/96.40% and SWEET's 32.32% (HumanEval-S)/41.46% (MBPP-S) -- the paper reports SWEET's TPR is 61.59% lower than MATRIX's on average. FPR for MATRIX ranges 0.00-3.05% (dual-channel, 'watermark present if either channel detects'), dropping under the stricter both-channel-required variant (MATRIX-Strict), e.g. to 0.00-0.42%. Message accuracy (Table VII, simplified (4,2,1) BCH): MATRIX achieves 100.00% MsgAcc across almost all datasets/bit patterns, versus ACW's occasional drops (e.g. 97.30% on HumanEval-G for message 0101). Applicability: MATRIX's embeddable sample count (Code-Num) is ~6x ACW's on APPS-G (2249 vs 372) and ~2x on HumanEval-G (70 vs 37) and MBPP-G (295 vs 148). A supplementary (7,4) BCH experiment on a 760-sample APPS subset: MATRIX watermarked 361 files at 100% TPR, 0.3% FPR, 100% MsgAcc, versus ACW watermarking only 3 files. Robustness (Figure 3, HumanEval/MBPP): under VA, MATRIX holds 96-98.67% TPR vs ACW's 72.00% and SWEET's 20.98%; under RA, MATRIX holds 92-98% vs sharp degradation for both baselines; under the hardest attack, LLM rewriting (LA), MATRIX achieves 53.85% (HumanEval) and 51.05% (MBPP), about 5-10 points above ACW and >3x SWEET; under FA (black reformatting), MATRIX retains 97-100% TPR while ACW and SWEET degrade sharply. Fidelity (Table VIII): MATRIX's unit-test pass-rate drops only 0.14% on APPS and 0% on HumanEval/MBPP, versus ACW's <0.2% and SWEET's up to 7.60% (MBPP-S) / 2.44% (HumanEval-S). Overhead (Table V): mean insertion time is 0.206s (MATRIX) vs 16.425s (ACW, which calls a Sourcery API) and 0.302s (SWEET). Cross-organization attribution (Table IV): TPRs typically above 95%, reaching 100% on several datasets, with 3-5% variation across organizations attributed to minor overlap between distinct parity-check matrices' encoding spaces.
core_contribution: MATRIX is a code watermarking framework for LLM-generated Python code that formulates watermark embedding as solving a constrained parity-check matrix equation system (M · r ≡ c mod 2, with grouped-state threshold constraints) over a state vector of 'anchor points'. It uses two independent, mutually-backing-up embedding channels: a 'formal channel' (semantics-preserving syntactic transformations, e.g. loop-to-comprehension, each reversible) and a 'natural channel' (variable-renaming schemes: underline suffix, initial capitalization, contextual suffix). The identity message m is first BCH-encoded into a longer watermark sequence w (with a random choice among codewords within the BCH error-correcting radius, to avoid a fixed one-to-one m-to-w mapping), then w is mapped via the parity-check system to a binary state vector over anchor points, and each anchor's forward or reverse transformation is applied according to its state. Detection re-derives anchor states from static analysis of the (possibly modified) code, reconstructs the group state vector, and checks the parity-check equations for either channel. Because different provenance layers (e.g. organization vs individual) need only swap the parity-check matrix M, not the transformation rule sets, the same encoding supports multi-layer/hierarchical attribution without redesigning the scheme.
threat_model: Stated explicitly in Section II-B. The watermark is embedded into LLM-generated code before distribution, so the attacker never sees the unwatermarked version. The attacker knows the high-level framework (dual-channel + parity-check design) but not the concrete transformation rules, anchor definitions, or the (secret) anchor-sorting function -- the paper argues full rule knowledge would let an adversary deterministically strip the watermark. The attacker receives only watermarked code and tries to remove/corrupt the watermark while preserving functionality, to evade attribution/responsibility. Four concrete attacker capabilities are evaluated: (1) Variable Renaming Attack (VA) -- random renaming of identifiers; (2) Refactoring Attack (RA) -- rule-based refactoring via pyrefact; (3) LLM-based Rewriting Attack (LA) -- full code regeneration by GPT-4o; (4) Reformat Attack (FA) -- code style normalization via black. A separate indistinguishability threat is a statistical adversary who observes multiple watermarked variants of the same code (with different embedded messages) and tries to infer transformation-to-bit correlations from anchor-activation patterns.
limitations: The paper's own text surfaces several limitations rather than claiming a strictly dominant method: (1) the dual-channel 'OR' detection rule that boosts TPR simultaneously raises FPR, which the authors address only by offering a stricter 'MATRIX-Strict' (AND) variant as an explicit accuracy/false-positive trade-off, not a free win; (2) some watermarks on the formal channel are undetected due to 'edge cases in static analysis' causing anchor-state transformation to fail; (3) under the hardest attack tested, LLM-based rewriting (GPT-4o), detection TPR falls to roughly 51-54%, i.e., barely better than a coin flip for binary presence detection, though still ahead of both baselines; (4) applicability is fundamentally bounded by the number of available anchor points in short/clean LLM-generated code, which the paper itself identifies as the reason ACW's coverage is so much lower, implying MATRIX's own applicability could similarly degrade on very short snippets (Case 3 in Algorithm 1 falls back to a fixed organization codeword with a unit parity-check matrix, weakening the message-carrying capacity); (5) evaluation is confined to Python code from GPT-4 and StarCoder on three benchmarks (MBPP, APPS, HumanEval) with code snippets under 5 lines excluded, so generalization to other languages or very short code is untested; (6) the 'secret anchor-sorting function' and rule-set secrecy are load-bearing security assumptions -- the threat model explicitly requires them to remain hidden from the attacker, and the paper does not evaluate a stronger adversary with knowledge of the sorting function.

quantitative_results: MATRIX TPR: APPS-G 99.64%, HumanEval-G 99.39%, MBPP-G 100.00%, HumanEval-S 96.95%, MBPP-S 100.00%
MATRIX FPR: APPS-G 1.34%, HumanEval-G 3.05%, MBPP-G 0.85%, HumanEval-S 0.00%, MBPP-S 2.20% (MATRIX-Strict FPR lower: 0.42/0.00/0.00/0.00/0.00%)
SWEET TPR on HumanEval-S 32.32%, on MBPP-S 41.46% -- 61.59% lower than MATRIX on average
ACW TPR: APPS-G 98.82%, HumanEval-G 99.39%, MBPP-G 99.79%, HumanEval-S 96.95%, MBPP-S 96.40%
MsgAcc (BCH (4,2,1)): MATRIX 100.00% across nearly all datasets/messages; ACW as low as 97.30% (HumanEval-G, message 0101)
Embeddable sample counts (Code-Num): APPS-G ACW 372 vs MATRIX 2249 (~6x); HumanEval-G 37 vs 70 (~2x); MBPP-G 148 vs 295 (~2x)
Supplementary (7,4) BCH on 760-sample APPS subset: MATRIX watermarked 361 files (TPR 100%, FPR 0.3%, MsgAcc 100%) vs ACW watermarked only 3 files
Robustness TPR under Variable Renaming Attack: MATRIX 96-98.67% vs ACW 72.00% vs SWEET 20.98%
Robustness TPR under LLM-based Rewriting Attack (GPT-4o): MATRIX 53.85% (HumanEval), 51.05% (MBPP) -- 5-10 points above ACW, >3x SWEET
Robustness TPR under Reformat Attack (black): MATRIX 97-100%
Watermark insertion overhead: MATRIX 0.206s, ACW 16.425s, SWEET 0.302s
Cross-organization attribution TPR: typically >95%, up to 100.00% (e.g. MBPP-G O2), with 3-5% inter-organization variation
Fidelity degradation: MATRIX APPS 0.14% (HumanEval/MBPP 0%); ACW under 0.2%; SWEET up to 7.60% (MBPP-S), 2.44% (HumanEval-S)
Watermark applicability increase: 2-6x over ACW; robustness improvement of 7.70-26.67% (abstract) / 7.70-21.72% (intro) against various attacks
baselines_compared: SWEET (white-box, entropy-thresholded KGW-derived decoding-time green/red-list watermarking)
